request.question: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of an animated television series that was created by who?

title: Business Guy
Clown in the Dumps
Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse
Cartoon Wars Part II
The Simpsons Guy
Ricardio the Heart Guy
Adventure Time (season 1)
Lady & Peebles
Brian & Stewie
The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show
sentences: "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.
The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.

Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.
"Clown in the Dumps" is the season premiere of the twenty-sixth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons", and the 553rd episode of the series overall.
It first aired in the United States on the Fox network on September 28, 2014, with "The Simpsons Guy", a crossover episode of "Family Guy" with "The Simpsons", airing afterwards.
This episode was dedicated in memory of Louis Castellaneta, the father of "The Simpsons" voice actor Dan Castellaneta.
It was written by Joel H. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore, with Don Hertzfeldt directing a sequence in the opening titles.

Jeff Ross, Sarah Silverman and David Hyde Pierce guest starred as themselves, with Jackie Mason and Kelsey Grammer reprising their respective roles as Rabbi Krustofski and Sideshow Bob, while Maurice LaMarche voiced several minor characters.

Family Guy: Back to the Multiverse is an action-adventure game that was released for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 on November 20, 2012, in North America, November 21, 2012, in Australia and November 23, 2012, in Europe.
The game is based on the American animated television series "Family Guy", most notably the episode "Road to the Multiverse", and is also a continuation of the episode "The Big Bang Theory".
This game also features the return of Stewie's evil half-brother Bertram, who was killed in the show.
"Back to the Multiverse" is the first "Family Guy" console game since "Family Guy Video Game!

When the game was available for pre-order, people who pre-ordered the game received a special level, based on "", another video game based on the "Aliens" trademark also owned by 20th Century Fox, which was released the next February to similar negative reception.
"Cartoon Wars Part II" is the fourth episode in the tenth season of the American animated television series "South Park".
The 143rd episode of the series overall, it first aired on Comedy Central in the United States on April 12, 2006.

After "Cartoon Wars Part I", it is the second part of a two-episode story-arc, which focuses on Cartman's efforts to get the television series "Family Guy" cancelled, by exploiting fears of retaliation by Muslims to an impending "Family Guy" episode in which the Islamic prophet Muhammad will appear, in violation of some interpretations of Muslim law.
Kyle instead urges the president of the network airing "Family Guy", Fox, to air the episode in an exercise of free speech.
"The Simpsons Guy" is the first episode of the thirteenth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and the 232nd overall episode.
"The Simpsons Guy" is a 45-minute-long crossover with "The Simpsons", and was written by Patrick Meighan and directed by Peter Shin.
It originally aired in the United States on September 28, 2014, on Fox, where both "The Simpsons" and "Family Guy" have aired since their respective debuts.
"Ricardio the Heart Guy" is the seventh episode of the first season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.
The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.
The first season of "Adventure Time", an American animated television series created by Pendleton Ward, premiered on Cartoon Network on April 5, 2010 and concluded on September 27, 2010.
The season was produced by Cartoon Network Studios and Frederator Studios.
The series is based on a short produced for Frederator's Nicktoons Network animation incubator series "Random!
Cartoons".
The season follows the adventures of Finn, a human boy, and his best friend and adoptive brother Jake, a dog with magical powers to change shape and size at will.

Finn and Jake live in the post-apocalyptic Land of Ooo, where they interact with the other main characters of the show: Princess Bubblegum, The Ice King, Marceline the Vampire Queen, Lumpy Space Princess, and BMO.
"Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".
The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.
It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.
The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.
"Brian & Stewie" is the 17th episode of the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy", and 150th episode of the series overall.
It originally aired on Fox in the United States on May 2, 2010.
The episode features anthropomorphic dog Brian and baby Stewie after they are accidentally trapped inside a bank vault over a weekend.
The two are ultimately forced to reveal their true feelings about each other, and eventually go on to question each other's existence and purpose in life.
Brian and Stewie become even closer to each other as time goes on, and climactically help each other survive being trapped inside the vault.

The bottle episode breaks from the show's usual set-up, and is the only episode of the series not to feature any music or use any cutaway gags with Brian and Stewie being the only two characters featured in the entire episode.
In repeats of the episode there is no main title sequence, nor is any music played over the end credits.
"The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show" is the fourteenth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "The Simpsons".
It originally aired on the Fox network in the United States on February 9, 1997.
In the episode, "The Itchy & Scratchy Show" attempts to regain viewers by introducing a new character named Poochie, whose voice is provided by Homer.
The episode is largely self-referential and satirizes the world of television production, fans of "The Simpsons", and the series itself.
It was written by David X. Cohen and directed by Steven Dean Moore.

Alex Rocco is a credited guest voice as Roger Meyers, Jr. for the third and final time (having previously provided the character's voice in "Itchy & Scratchy & Marge" and "The Day the Violence Died"); Phil Hartman also guest stars as Troy McClure.
Poochie would become a minor recurring character and Comic Book Guy's catchphrase, "Worst episode ever", is introduced in this episode.
With "The Itchy & Scratchy & Poochie Show", the show's 167th episode, "The Simpsons" surpassed "The Flintstones" in the number of episodes produced for a prime-time animated series.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of an animated television series that was created by who?
hop1_docs: Ricardio the Heart Guy | "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is the seventh episode of the first season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".  The episode was written and storyboarded by Bert Youn and Sean Jimenez, from a story by Merriwether Williams, Tim McKeon, and Adam Muto.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on April 26, 2010.  The episode guest stars George Takei as the title character, Ricardio.

Lady & Peebles | "Lady & Peebles" is the nineteenth episode of the fourth season of the American animated television series "Adventure Time".  The episode was written and storyboarded by Cole Sanchez and Rebecca Sugar, from a story by Patrick McHale, Kent Osborne, and Pendleton Ward.  It originally aired on Cartoon Network on August 20, 2012.  The episode guest stars George Takei as Ricardio.

SantApprentice | SantApprentice is an animated television series in 50 episodes of 12 minutes and two 26 minute episodes created by Jan Van Rijsselberge and made by Alphanim (now Gaumont Animation) in 2006.  The holiday series follows the adventures of Nicolas, a young orphan boy from Sydney, Australia, who is a pure of heart and believes in Santa Claus as he is Santa's Apprentice.  Nicolas's job is to achieve his tasks to become full-fledged Santa.

List of Family Guy writers | The following is a list of writers who have worked on the Fox animated television series "Family Guy" in the order of first credited episode (by broadcast).  As of 7 December 2014, 60 people have been credited with writing or co-writing at least one episode of "Family Guy".

You Can't Do That on Television, Peter | "You Can't Do That on Television, Peter" is the eighteenth episode of the tenth season of the animated comedy series, "Family Guy".  The episode originally aired on Fox in the United States on April 1, 2012.  In the episode, Peter creates and stars in a children's TV show after his favourite show is cancelled.  Meanwhile, Meg shadows Dr. Hartman at the Quahog hospital.

Business Guy | "Business Guy" is the ninth episode in the eighth season of the American animated television series "Family Guy".  It originally aired on Fox in the United States on December 13, 2009.  The episode centers on Peter as he assumes temporary control of his father-in-law Carter Pewterschmidt's billion-dollar empire after he suffers a heart attack and is incapacitated in a coma.  Peter immediately becomes power hungry, however, and develops several ridiculous items, prompting Lois and Carter, after he recovers from his coma, to try to trick him into relinquishing control of the company.

Steve Marmel | Steve Marmel (born December 17, 1964) is an American television writer and producer who has worked on many animated TV series, including "The Fairly OddParents", "I Am Weasel", "Danny Phantom", "Family Guy" and "Yin Yang Yo! ".  During his work on "The Fairly OddParents" he frequently co-wrote episodes with Butch Hartman.  Marmel also created the series "Sonny with a Chance" as well as the series "Mech-X4".
hop1_titles: You Can't Do That on Television, Peter
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage 1 states "'Ricardio the Heart Guy' is ... an episode of the American animated television series 'Adventure Time'." Bridge entity: Pendleton Ward (named in passage 2 as a story contributor on an Adventure Time episode) and the series "Adventure Time" should be investigated. Missing fact: the name of the person who "created" Adventure Time is not given in the passages. Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "Adventure Time" main article (e.g., Wikipedia) and check the "Created by" / infobox field for the creator's name; if needed, 2) open the "Pendleton Ward" biography page to confirm whether he is credited as the series creator. [[ ## completed ## ]]
response.hop2_query: "Adventure Time created by" site:wikipedia.org OR "Who created Adventure Time" Pendleton Ward site:wikipedia.org
hop2_docs: Wikidumper.org | WikiDumper.org is a website created by Clifford A. Pickover that promises to permanently record a snapshot of the "best of the Wikipedia rejects", articles that are slated for deletion at the site.  WikiDumper was launched on November 4, 2006, and accepts user submissions.  Although the site doesn't specify its criteria for inclusion, many of its articles don't cite their sources.  The site has been criticized as likely to be less accurate than Wikipedia.

Pendleton Ward | Pendleton Ward (born September 23, 1982) is an American animator, screenwriter, producer, and voice actor who works for Cartoon Network Studios and Frederator Studios.  He created the Emmy Award-winning series "Adventure Time" (2010) and the internet series "Bravest Warriors" (2012).  Ward is a graduate of the CalArts Animation Program.  He grew up in San Antonio, Texas and currently resides in Los Angeles.

Bad Timing (Adventure Time) | "Bad Timing" is the forty-ninth episode from the fifth season of "Adventure Time", an animated television series.  It was written by Pendleton Ward, who created the show, and Kent Osborne, its head of story from a story the developed along with Jack Pendarvis and the current showrunner, Adam Muto.  In the episode, Lumpy Space Princess falls in love with a former mate (Mark Proksch) from secondary school.  When she suspects she has lost her date to Princess Bubblegum, she attempts to wield time travel to win back his affections.

Adventure Time (pilot) | "Adventure Time" is an animated short created by Pendleton Ward, as well as the pilot to the Cartoon Network series of the same name.  The short follows the adventures of Pen (voiced by Zack Shada), a human boy, and his best friend Jake (voiced by John DiMaggio), a dog with magical powers to change shape and grow and shrink at will.  In this episode, Pen and Jake have to rescue Princess Bubblegum (voiced by Paige Moss) from the antagonistic Ice King (voiced by John Kassir).

Marceline the Vampire Queen | Marceline the Vampire Queen is a character in the American animated television series "Adventure Time" created by Pendleton Ward.  She is voiced by Olivia Olson in most appearances, by Ava Acres as a child and by Cloris Leachman as an old woman.  Marceline is a fun-loving 1,000-year-old vampire queen.  The name "Marceline" is based on the middle name of a childhood friend of Ward's, Marie.  The artistic design for Marceline was created by Ward, with small changes and additions added by Phil Rynda, former lead-character designer and prop designer for "Adventure Time".  Unlike a traditional vampire, Marceline does not need to drink blood to survive; rather, she eats the color red.  Marceline is a musician who plays an electric bass that she made from her family's heirloom battle-axe.

Robert Jessup | Robert Jessup (born July 18, 1952) is an American painter.  Although currently working in the abstract style he painted for most of his career in the figurative style (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Figurative_art), particularly in large triptych panels (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triptych).
response.summary_2: "Ricardio the Heart Guy" is an episode of Adventure Time, which was created by animator Pendleton Ward. Multiple passages here explicitly credit Pendleton Ward as the creator (including mention of his creating the series and its pilot).
